findings.description: Zip Slip in TarUnArchiver: archive entry name flows to extractFile() without path traversal check, allowing arbitrary file write outside the destination directory
findings.code_snippet:
extractFile( sourceFile, destDirectory, tis, te.getName(), te.getModTime(), te.isDirectory(),
                                 te.getMode() != 0 ? te.getMode() : null, symlinkDestination );
findings.reasoning: In TarUnArchiver.execute(), te.getName() is retrieved from TarArchiveEntry and passed directly to extractFile() as the entryName parameter. Inside AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() at line 309, the file is resolved via FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) without any path containment check. There is no verification that the resolved path stays within destDirectory. An attacker can craft a malicious tar archive with entries named like ../../etc/cron.d/evil to write files outside the extraction directory. No getCanonicalPath() comparison is performed before writing.

evidence.code_context: TarUnArchiver.execute() line 110-114: te.getName() passed to extractFile(). AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() line 309: final File f = FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) - NO path containment check. Then out = new FileOutputStream(f) writes to attacker-controlled path. grep confirms getCanonicalPath() only exists in DirectoryArchiver line 87, not in AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile().

exploit_payload.code_snippet: TarArchiveOutputStream taos = new TarArchiveOutputStream(new FileOutputStream("evil.tar")); TarArchiveEntry entry = new TarArchiveEntry("../../etc/cron.d/backdoor"); byte[] content = "* * * * * root /tmp/shell.sh".getBytes(); entry.setSize(content.length); taos.putArchiveEntry(entry); taos.write(content); taos.closeArchiveEntry(); taos.close(); // TarUnArchiver.execute() then writes file to /etc/cron.d/backdoor
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious tar archive with entry names containing ../ sequences escaping the extraction directory, enabling arbitrary file write
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a tar archive that is extracted by the application using TarUnArchiver

findings.description: Zip Slip in AbstractZipUnArchiver: archive entry name flows to extractFile() without path traversal check, allowing arbitrary file write outside the destination directory
findings.code_snippet: extractFile( sourceFile, destDirectory, inputStream, name, time, isDirectory, mode, symlinkDestination );
findings.reasoning: In AbstractZipUnArchiver.execute(), fileInfo.getName() (derived from ZipArchiveEntry.getName()) is passed to extractFileIfIncluded() which directly calls extractFile() without any path containment check. Inside AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() at line 309, the file is resolved via FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) without verifying the result stays within destDirectory. An attacker can craft a malicious zip archive with entries named like ../../etc/passwd to overwrite arbitrary files. No getCanonicalPath() comparison is performed anywhere in this code path.

evidence.code_context: AbstractZipUnArchiver execute() lines 178-185: fileInfo.getName() passed to extractFileIfIncluded(). extractFileIfIncluded() at line 222: directly calls extractFile(sourceFile, destDirectory, inputStream, name, time, isDirectory, mode, symlinkDestination). AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() line 309: final File f = FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) - NO containment check, then writes file. The second execute() overload at line 251 also passes ze.getName() without checking for traversal sequences.

exploit_payload.code_snippet: ZipArchiveOutputStream zaos = new ZipArchiveOutputStream(new FileOutputStream("evil.zip")); ZipArchiveEntry entry = new ZipArchiveEntry("../../etc/cron.d/backdoor"); byte[] content = "* * * * * root /tmp/shell.sh".getBytes(); entry.setSize(content.length); zaos.putArchiveEntry(entry); zaos.write(content); zaos.closeArchiveEntry(); zaos.close(); // AbstractZipUnArchiver.execute() then writes file to /etc/cron.d/backdoor
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious zip archive with entry names containing ../ sequences escaping the extraction directory, enabling arbitrary file write
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a zip/jar archive that is extracted by the application using AbstractZipUnArchiver

findings.description: Path traversal in DirectoryArchiver: archive entry name concatenated with destination path without containment check, allowing files to be written outside the destination directory
findings.reasoning: In DirectoryArchiver.execute(), the archive entry name f.getName() is retrieved and concatenated with the canonical path of destDirectory. While destDirectory is canonicalized, the entry name itself is not checked for path traversal sequences. If f.getName() contains '../' sequences (e.g., '../../etc/cron.d/evil'), the resulting fileName will escape destDirectory. The resulting file path is then passed to copyFile() which creates a FileOutputStream at that location, enabling arbitrary file write. There is no check that the final path starts with destDir after concatenation.

evidence.code_context: DirectoryArchiver.execute() lines 80-99: String fileName = f.getName(); final String destDir = destDirectory.getCanonicalPath(); fileName = destDir + File.separator + fileName; ... copyFile(f, fileName). The archive entry name from ArchiveEntry.getName() (line 86) is concatenated directly with the canonical destDir path. No check like fileName.startsWith(destDir) is performed after concatenation. copyFile() then creates a File(vPath) and writes content to it.
evidence.notes: The destDirectory is canonicalized (line 87) but the entry name is not validated - if it contains '../' the final path escapes the destination. This is the archiver (writes to dir) variant of Zip Slip, triggered when archive entries from an archive source are copied to a directory.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker controls archive entry names that contain ../ sequences, causing DirectoryArchiver to write files outside the intended destination directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must control archive entry names fed into DirectoryArchiver
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write via path traversal

findings.description: Zip Slip in AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile: entry name used in FileUtils.resolveFile() without path containment check, enabling arbitrary file write
findings.code_snippet: final File f = FileUtils.resolveFile( dir, entryName );
findings.reasoning: AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() uses FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) to compute the target file path from the archive entry name, but performs no subsequent containment check (e.g., no f.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(dir.getCanonicalPath())). This means if entryName contains path traversal sequences like '../../etc/passwd', the resolved file f will point outside the intended extraction directory dir. The method then proceeds to create parent directories and write content to f via FileOutputStream without validation. This is the root cause vulnerability that affects all unarchiver subclasses (TarUnArchiver, ZipUnArchiver, etc.).

evidence.notes: The comment in the code 'Symlinks re-evaluate back to the original file here. Unsure if this is a good thing...' acknowledges awareness of symlink issues but no path traversal fix was applied.

exploit_payload.scenario: Any archive (tar/zip/jar) with entry names containing ../ causes AbstractUnArchiver to write files to arbitrary filesystem locations outside the extraction directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply an archive processed by any AbstractUnArchiver subclass
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write (Zip Slip) - root cause

findings.description: Zip Slip in AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs: archive entry name used in File construction without path traversal check
findings.reasoning: In AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs(), the `dir` string is derived from archive entry names via AddedDirs.asStringStack() which extracts path components from the entry name. These dir strings are passed directly to new File(baseDir, dir) or new File(dir) without any path containment check. If the archive entry name contains path traversal sequences (../../etc), the resulting File object can point outside the intended archive base directory. This is the archiver-side variant where entry names from an existing archive being re-packed can escape their intended location.

evidence.notes: The taint flows from ArchiveEntry.getName() through AbstractZipArchiver.addResources() -> addParentDirs() -> File(baseDir, dir) at line 454 and File(dir) at line 458.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker controls archive entry names that contain ../ sequences, causing AbstractZipArchiver to read from filesystem locations outside the intended archive base when building parent directory entries
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must control archive entry names processed by AbstractZipArchiver

findings.description: Path traversal in DirectoryArchiver.copyFile: archive entry name used in File construction via vPath without path containment check
findings.code_snippet: final File outFile = new File( vPath );
findings.reasoning: In DirectoryArchiver.copyFile(), the parameter vPath is constructed in execute() as destDir + File.separator + f.getName() where f.getName() comes from the archive entry. The vPath string is used directly to construct a File object (new File(vPath)) without any check that it stays within the intended destination directory. Since f.getName() can contain path traversal sequences like '../../etc', vPath resolves to a path outside destDir. The resulting outFile is then used to write content via ResourceUtils.copyFile(), enabling arbitrary file write. Although destDir is canonicalized, there is no startsWith check on the final computed path.

evidence.notes: The flow goes: ArchiveEntry.getName() -> execute() builds fileName -> copyFile(f, fileName) -> new File(vPath) -> ResourceUtils.copyFile() -> FileOutputStream(outFile). This is flagged by query 20260329165359 as archive entry to FileOutputStream.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker controls archive entry names containing ../ sequences, causing DirectoryArchiver.copyFile to write content to arbitrary filesystem locations
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must control archive entry names processed by DirectoryArchiver
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write via path traversal

findings.description: Path traversal in ResourceUtils.copyFile: archive entry name flows to FileOutputStream constructor without path traversal validation
findings.code_snippet: output = new FileOutputStream( outFile );
findings.reasoning: ResourceUtils.copyFile(InputStream input, File outFile) opens a FileOutputStream on the outFile parameter. The outFile is constructed in DirectoryArchiver.copyFile() as new File(vPath) where vPath = destDir + separator + entryName, and entryName comes from an ArchiveEntry whose name derives from an archive entry name (which can be attacker-controlled). Since no path containment check is performed before calling ResourceUtils.copyFile(), the outFile can point to an arbitrary filesystem location outside the intended directory. This is the final sink in the taint flow chain from archive entry name to file write.

exploit_payload.scenario: Archive entry names with ../ sequences flow through DirectoryArchiver into ResourceUtils.copyFile, enabling overwrite of arbitrary files via FileOutputStream
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must control archive entry names processed by DirectoryArchiver which calls ResourceUtils.copyFile
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write via path traversal

findings.description: Zip Slip in JarArchiver: archive entry getName() used in same method as File construction without path traversal check
findings.code_snippet:
ZipArchiveEntry ze = entries.nextElement();
                    String name = ze.getName();
findings.reasoning: In JarArchiver, archive entry names from ZipArchiveEntry.getName() are retrieved and used in the same method that constructs File objects or performs file operations. The entry name `name` from a ZipArchiveEntry is used directly without path traversal validation. Since JarArchiver extends AbstractZipArchiver which itself passes entry names to File constructors (as seen in addParentDirs()), the same lack of path containment check applies. A malicious JAR with traversal-containing entry names could escape the intended directory during indexing/archiving operations.

evidence.code_context: JarArchiver lines 780-810: ZipArchiveEntry ze = entries.nextElement(); String name = ze.getName(); used in same method context with file operations. The entry name is checked against specific strings (META_INF_NAME, INDEX_NAME, MANIFEST_NAME) but general path traversal sequences are not blocked. Names containing ../ are not filtered.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious JAR archive with entry names containing ../ sequences that are used in JarArchiver file operations without path validation
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a JAR archive processed by JarArchiver
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal in JAR archive processing

findings.description: Path traversal exposure in AbstractUnArchiver.isSelected: archive entry getName() used without path validation in file selector check
findings.code_snippet: if ( !fileSelector.isSelected( fileInfo ) )
findings.reasoning: In AbstractUnArchiver.isSelected(), archive entry names from getName() are passed to fileSelector.isSelected(fileInfo) without path traversal sanitization. While this is a selection/filtering step rather than a direct file write, the entry name containing traversal sequences is passed to the file selector which may make decisions based on the raw name. More critically, isSelected() returning true allows the same unsanitized entry name to flow onward to extractFile() where the actual Zip Slip occurs. This is part of the taint flow chain identified in Query 3.

evidence.code_context: AbstractUnArchiver.isSelected() line 252: if (!fileSelector.isSelected(fileInfo)) - fileInfo.getName() returns the raw archive entry name including any traversal sequences. The isSelected check does not sanitize the name, it only determines whether to include/exclude the entry. If it returns true (entry is selected), the unsanitized entry name proceeds to extractFile() in TarUnArchiver/AbstractZipUnArchiver with no further validation.

exploit_payload.scenario: Traversal entry names pass through isSelected() unchecked and flow directly to extractFile() causing arbitrary file write
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Default configuration with no file selector, or file selector that does not block traversal paths
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal - intermediate step in Zip Slip chain

findings.description: Zip Slip in AbstractZipUnArchiver second execute() overload: entry name used in extractFileIfIncluded without path traversal check, startsWith check on raw entry name is bypassable
findings.reasoning: In the second execute() overload of AbstractZipUnArchiver (which takes a path and outputDirectory), ze.getName() is checked with startsWith(path) - this is a String.startsWith() check, not a path-component-aware check. This is bypassable: e.g. if path is 'foo/', an entry named 'foo/../../etc/passwd' would pass the check but resolve to /etc/passwd. After the check, ze.getName() is passed directly to extractFileIfIncluded() which calls extractFile() -> FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) with no further containment validation. The same Zip Slip vulnerability exists here as in the main execute() overload.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a zip with entries starting with an expected prefix followed by ../ sequences, bypassing the startsWith check and writing files outside the output directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a zip archive processed by the AbstractZipUnArchiver path-filtered extraction method
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write (Zip Slip) - bypassable prefix check

findings.description: Path traversal via archive entry name in AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile - no canonicalization or boundary check
findings.code_snippet: final File f = FileUtils.resolveFile( dir, entryName );
findings.reasoning: The `entryName` parameter comes directly from archive entry names (e.g., tar or zip entry names like `../../etc/passwd`). `FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName)` resolves the entry name relative to the destination directory, but there is NO subsequent check that the resulting path `f` is still within `dir`. An attacker crafting a malicious archive with entry names containing `../` sequences can cause files to be written outside the intended extraction directory. No canonicalization or `startsWith` containment check is performed after the resolve operation. This is the core extraction method called by both TarUnArchiver and ZipUnArchiver for all file extraction operations.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: entryName comes from tar entry (te.getName()) in TarUnArchiver.execute at line 113 or from zip entry (ze.getName()) in AbstractZipUnArchiver. Neither caller validates the entry name before passing it to extractFile. FileUtils.resolveFile resolves paths but does not prevent directory traversal.
evidence.notes: Only one call to getCanonicalPath exists in the entire codebase (DirectoryArchiver.java:87), not in any unarchiver path. No startsWith containment check exists in this method or callers.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious tar/zip archive with entry names containing ../ sequences to write files outside the intended extraction directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to supply a malicious archive file that the application will extract

findings.description: Symlink path traversal in AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile - symlink destination from archive is used without validation
findings.code_snippet: SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink( f, new File( symlinkDestination ) );
findings.reasoning: The `symlinkDestination` comes directly from the archive entry's link name (e.g., TarArchiveEntry.getLinkName() in TarUnArchiver, or the zip symlink content). This value is used directly as the symlink target without any validation. An attacker can create an archive with a symlink entry whose target is an absolute path (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) or a path traversal sequence (e.g., `../../../../etc/shadow`). When the archive is extracted, a symlink is created pointing to the attacker-controlled location. Furthermore, if other archive entries are then written relative to this symlink (e.g., a directory entry followed by a file entry within it), files can escape the extraction directory entirely via the symlink chain.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: te.getLinkName() flows directly from archive entry to SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink with no intermediate validation. The symlink target is completely attacker-controlled.
evidence.notes: This is a classic 'TarSlip' / symlink attack. Combined with the path traversal in extractFile, an attacker can: 1) Create a symlink entry pointing outside the extraction dir, 2) Create a subsequent file entry that writes through the symlink. No check for absolute paths or traversal in symlinkDestination.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a tar archive with a symlink entry pointing to an absolute path, then writes files through the symlink to escape the extraction directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a malicious archive. Symlinks must be supported by the filesystem.
exploit_payload.category: Symlink-based path traversal / arbitrary file write

findings.description: Path traversal in TarUnArchiver.execute - tar entry name and symlink destination passed without validation to extractFile
findings.code_snippet:
extractFile( sourceFile, destDirectory, tis, te.getName(), te.getModTime(), te.isDirectory(),
                                 te.getMode() != 0 ? te.getMode() : null, symlinkDestination );
findings.reasoning: The tar entry name `te.getName()` is passed directly to `extractFile` without any validation for path traversal sequences (e.g., `../`, absolute paths). Similarly, `symlinkDestination` derived from `te.getLinkName()` is passed directly. There is no check for `..` sequences, absolute paths, or containment within the destination directory before this call. The `extractFile` method in `AbstractUnArchiver` uses `FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName)` which resolves the path but does NOT perform containment checks. A malicious tar file with entries like `../../etc/cron.d/malicious` would be extracted outside the target directory.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: te.getName() and te.getLinkName() come directly from the TarArchiveEntry parsed from the tar file stream. No sanitization between reading from archive and passing to extractFile.
evidence.notes: Line 110 (getName) is just the isSelected check which is a file selector filter, not a security boundary check. Lines 112-113 show the symlink check and direct passthrough.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies a tar file with entry names containing ../ sequences that escape the extraction destination directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker controls the tar archive being extracted

findings.description: Bypassable path prefix check using String.startsWith() in AbstractZipUnArchiver.execute - no canonicalization before check
findings.code_snippet: if ( ze.getName().startsWith( path ) )
findings.reasoning: This `startsWith(path)` check in the `execute(String path, File outputDirectory)` overload is intended to filter which zip entries to extract. However, this is a string-based prefix check on the raw entry name (not canonicalized), which serves only as an entry filter (to extract a subset of entries), NOT as a security boundary to prevent path traversal. After this check passes, the entry name still flows unmodified into `extractFileIfIncluded` -> `extractFile` -> `FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName)` with no containment check. An entry named `prefix/../../../etc/passwd` would pass the `startsWith(prefix)` check while resolving to outside the destination directory. Even without path traversal, this check is not a security boundary - it's simply filtering which entries to process.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ze.getName() passes startsWith check then flows to extractFileIfIncluded -> extractFile -> FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName). No canonicalization between check and use.
evidence.notes: The CodeQL query correctly identifies that no getCanonicalPath() is used in this method. The startsWith here is a content filter, not a security check, making it vulnerable per the 'String-based startsWith() check on a non-canonical path' true positive indicator.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a zip archive with entry names that pass the startsWith filter but contain ../ sequences to escape the destination directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The execute(String path, File outputDirectory) overload must be called (requires a path prefix argument); attacker controls the zip archive
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal bypass via non-canonical startsWith check

findings.description: File construction in DirectoryArchiver.execute - false positive, archiver not unarchiver, ArchiveEntry names are server-side controlled
findings.code_snippet:
final String destDir = destDirectory.getCanonicalPath();
                fileName = destDir + File.separator + fileName;
findings.reasoning: DirectoryArchiver is an Archiver (not an UnArchiver) that copies files to a destination directory. The `ArchiveEntry` names come from server-side configured file sets (added via addFile/addDirectory calls), not from user-supplied archive entries. Additionally, the destination directory is canonicalized via `getCanonicalPath()`. While string concatenation without subsequent canonical check could theoretically be bypassed by `f.getName()` containing `../`, the source of `f.getName()` is the developer-controlled `ArchiveEntry` name set during build configuration, not attacker-controlled input. This is build tooling, not an extraction path traversal vulnerability.

evidence.notes: ArchiveEntry.getName() returns names set programmatically by the build tool. Not derived from untrusted archive content. DirectoryArchiver extends AbstractArchiver, an archiver not an unarchiver.

findings.description: File construction in JarArchiver.grabFilesAndDirs - false positive, names only collected for index generation, no filesystem write
findings.code_snippet: File zipFile = new File( file );
findings.reasoning: The `grabFilesAndDirs` method constructs a `File` object from a string parameter `file` to check if it exists and open it as a zip file. The entry names read from the zip (`ze.getName()`) are only added to `dirs` and `files` lists for JAR index generation purposes. The entry names are never used to create output files on the filesystem. This is not a path traversal vulnerability - it's just reading zip entry names into in-memory lists. The `new File(file)` on line 766 uses the `file` parameter which is the path to the jar being indexed, not a user-controlled entry name.

evidence.notes: Entry names ze.getName() flow only to dirSet.add() and files.add() - in-memory collections. No FileOutputStream or file creation from entry names.

findings.description: File construction in AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs - false positive, archive creation not extraction, internal directory stack logic
findings.reasoning: The `addParentDirs` method is part of archive CREATION (AbstractZipArchiver, not an unarchiver). It creates `File` objects from `dir` entries popped from an internal `addedDirs.asStringStack(entry)` call. The `dir` values come from archive entry names that were already added to the archive by the developer/build tool, not from attacker-controlled archive entry names being extracted. The `File` objects created here are used as metadata sources for `PlexusIoResource` to be read and written INTO the archive, not written out to the filesystem from an untrusted archive. This is archive creation logic, not extraction.

evidence.notes: This method writes directory entries INTO a zip archive (zOut), not extracting FROM one. baseDir is a server-side path. dir values come from internal entry tracking (addedDirs stack), not user-supplied archive content.

findings.description: Symlink target from archive entry used without validation in TarUnArchiver - symlink destination not checked for absolute paths or traversal
findings.code_snippet: final String symlinkDestination = te.isSymbolicLink() ? te.getLinkName() : null;
findings.reasoning: The symlink destination `te.getLinkName()` is read directly from the tar archive entry and assigned to `symlinkDestination` without any validation. This value flows directly to `AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile` where it is used as the target of `SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(f, new File(symlinkDestination))`. No check is made for: (1) absolute paths in the symlink target (e.g., `/etc/passwd`), (2) path traversal sequences in the target (e.g., `../../../../etc`), or (3) whether the symlink target resolves within the extraction directory. This enables the "TarSlip" class of attacks where a malicious tar contains a symlink pointing outside the destination directory, which can then be followed by subsequent entries to write files to arbitrary locations.

evidence.notes: getLinkName() is a raw string from the tar archive. No isAbsolute() check, no canonicalization, no containment check on the target.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a tar archive where a symlink entry has an absolute path target, enabling subsequent archive entries to be written to attacker-controlled filesystem locations outside the extraction directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker controls the tar archive; filesystem must support symlinks

findings.description: File construction in TarUnArchiver.execute(String, File) - false positive, path is the source archive file path not a destination, server-side controlled
findings.code_snippet: execute( new File( path ), getDestDirectory() );
findings.reasoning: The `new File(path)` here constructs a File object from the `path` parameter in the `execute(String path, File outputDirectory)` method. This `path` is the path to the **source tar archive** to be extracted from, not a destination path or an archive entry name. The constructed File is passed as `sourceFile` to the main `execute(File sourceFile, File destDirectory)` method, where it is used to open the tar archive for reading. This is not a path traversal vulnerability - it's simply resolving the location of the input archive file, which is set programmatically by the build tool or application, not derived from archive entry names.

evidence.notes: The 'path' parameter here is the source archive file path (equivalent to getSourceFile()), not a destination path. It flows to execute(File sourceFile, File destDirectory) where it's used as TarArchiveInputStream source. Not attacker-controlled archive entry data.

findings.description: File construction in AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs line 458 - false positive, duplicate of line 454, archive creation not extraction
findings.code_snippet: f = new File( dir );
findings.reasoning: This is the else-branch of the same `addParentDirs` method already assessed at line 454. Both lines 454 and 458 construct `File` objects from `dir` (from internal `addedDirs` stack) during archive CREATION, not extraction. The `dir` values come from entry names already added to the archive by developer-controlled build configuration. The resulting `File` objects are used as source metadata (`PlexusIoResource`) to write directory entries INTO the zip archive, not to write files out to the filesystem from untrusted archive content. This is the same false positive as line 454.

evidence.notes: Same addParentDirs method as line 454. f is used as a resource source for writing INTO the archive (zOut), not writing out from it. Archive creation context, not extraction.

findings.description: getName() in TarUnArchiver.execute at line 110 - false positive, used only for file selector filtering not for path construction
findings.code_snippet: if ( isSelected( te.getName(), fileInfo ) )
findings.reasoning: The `te.getName()` at line 110 is used only in the `isSelected()` call which checks include/exclude file selectors (e.g., Ant-style patterns). This is a content filter to decide which entries to process, not a security boundary against path traversal. It does not construct file paths or perform any file I/O. The actual path construction and potential traversal vulnerability is at lines 112-113 (already submitted as tp). This line itself is not a vulnerability - it's just reading the entry name for selector matching.

evidence.notes: isSelected() performs include/exclude pattern matching. It is not a security control against path traversal. The real vulnerability is the unvalidated passthrough to extractFile at line 113.

findings.description: File construction in DirectoryArchiver.execute line 94 - false positive, duplicate of line 93, archiver not unarchiver, server-side controlled ArchiveEntry names
findings.code_snippet: fileName = destDir + File.separator + fileName;
findings.reasoning: This is the second line of the same two-line block in DirectoryArchiver.execute already assessed at line 93. `fileName` is constructed as `destDir + separator + f.getName()` where `destDir` comes from `destDirectory.getCanonicalPath()` and `f.getName()` comes from developer-controlled `ArchiveEntry` names (not from untrusted archive entries). This is part of the archive CREATION path that copies developer-configured resources to a destination directory. The ArchiveEntry names are set programmatically by the build configuration, not sourced from user-supplied archive files. Same false positive rationale as line 93.

evidence.notes: Lines 93-94 are a single operation. f is an ArchiveEntry from the build tool's resource iterator (server-side controlled). Not attacker-controlled archive content.
